title: Packet Loss Impact Guide
summary: Understand how packet loss affects throughput, real-time traffic, retransmissions, and user experience.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool helps estimate whether packet loss is likely to create noticeable performance or reliability issues.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch network constraints before they become field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when troubleshooting WAN links, wireless bridges, VPNs, voice/video traffic, storage replication, or unstable network paths.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-network validation.
Explaining capacity, latency, or reliability tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical network planning terms.

items.label: Packet Loss Rate
items.description: The percentage of packets that fail to arrive correctly.
items.whyItMatters: Small loss rates can still hurt sensitive workloads.
items.commonMistake: Treating 1% loss as harmless for every application.

items.label: Traffic Type
items.description: The workload affected by packet loss.
items.whyItMatters: Different traffic reacts differently to loss.
items.commonMistake: Using one loss threshold for every workload.

items.label: Latency
items.description: Delay across the path where loss occurs.
items.whyItMatters: Loss combined with latency can severely reduce throughput.
items.commonMistake: Checking loss without considering round-trip time.

items.label: Retransmission Behavior
items.description: Whether the protocol retransmits lost data.
items.whyItMatters: Retransmission can recover data but may slow performance.
items.commonMistake: Assuming lost real-time packets can always be recovered.

items.label: Link Utilization
items.description: How busy the link is when loss occurs.
items.whyItMatters: Loss during congestion often indicates a capacity or queueing issue.
items.commonMistake: Testing loss only when the link is quiet.

items.label: Impact Tolerance
items.description: How much degradation the workload can tolerate.
items.whyItMatters: Critical or real-time workloads need lower loss.
items.commonMistake: Accepting loss because basic browsing still works.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means loss is likely low enough for the workload. Watch means path quality should be reviewed. Risk means the workload may suffer degraded performance or instability.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumptions deserve review.
Risk: increase capacity, reduce load, simplify the path, or validate more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most network planning problems come from checking raw link speed while ignoring overhead, concurrency, peak traffic, latency, failover, and growth.
bullets: Sizing from theoretical maximums instead of usable throughput.
Ignoring protocol overhead, VPN overhead, or retransmission.
Sizing for average traffic and forgetting peak windows.
Failing to document assumptions before the network is built.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, carrier/service-provider limits, application testing, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Real throughput is usually lower than rated link speed.
Traffic mix and timing matter.
Latency, loss, and jitter can be as important as bandwidth.
A design with headroom is usually easier to support and expand.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, service-provider validation, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual network equipment and services being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations and application requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
